title: Docker: container exits immediately
solution_steps: 1. Check logs: `docker logs <container>`
2. Run interactively: `docker run -it <image> /bin/bash`
3. Ensure the CMD/ENTRYPOINT runs in foreground (not daemon)
4. For web apps: use CMD that doesn't fork to background
5. Check for missing environment variables
6. Use `docker run --rm -it <image> sh` to debug
code_example:
# Check why it exited
docker logs my-container
docker run --rm -it my-image sh
# Fix CMD in Dockerfile
CMD ["npm", "start"]  # not: npm start (runs in background shell)

title: Docker: port already in use
solution_steps: 1. Find process using the port: `netstat -ano | findstr :PORT` (Windows)
2. Stop the container: `docker stop <container>`
3. Change host port: `-p 8081:80` instead of `-p 8080:80`
4. Kill process: `taskkill /PID <pid> /F` (Windows)
5. Restart Docker Desktop
6. Check for WSL/VM using the port
code_example:
# Find what's using port 8080
netstat -ano | findstr :8080
# Stop all containers
docker stop $(docker ps -q)

title: Docker: image pull timeout / network
solution_steps: 1. Check internet connection
2. Configure Docker DNS: `{"dns": ["8.8.8.8", "1.1.1.1"]}` in daemon.json
3. Set HTTP proxy for Docker daemon
4. Use a different registry mirror
5. Restart Docker Desktop
6. Increase pull timeout in Docker settings

title: Docker: build cache invalidation
solution_steps: 1. Order layers from least to most frequently changing
2. Copy package.json BEFORE source code for dependency caching
3. Use `docker build --cache-from` for CI caching
4. Use `--no-cache` to force full rebuild
5. Use multi-stage builds to separate build/dev dependencies
6. Use `.dockerignore` to exclude unnecessary files
code_example:
# Optimized Dockerfile
FROM node:18 AS deps
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm install

FROM node:18 AS build
COPY --from=deps /app/node_modules ./node_modules
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

title: Docker Compose: service dependency ordering
solution_steps: 1. Use `depends_on` with `condition: service_healthy`
2. Add healthcheck to the dependency service
3. Use `wait-for-it.sh` script in entrypoint
4. Use `dockerize` tool for waiting
5. Use retry logic in application code
6. Docker Compose v3+ `depends_on` does NOT wait for readiness
code_example:
services:
  db:
    image: postgres:16
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U postgres"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 5
  app:
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy

title: Docker: volume mounting permissions
solution_steps: 1. Check host directory permissions: `ls -la /path/to/volume`
2. Use `:Z` or `:z` flags on SELinux systems
3. Use `user: "1000:1000"` in docker-compose to match host UID
4. Use named volumes instead of bind mounts for better compatibility
5. On Windows: enable shared drives in Docker settings
6. Ensure paths are absolute
code_example:
services:
  app:
    image: myapp
    user: "1000:1000"
    volumes:
      - ./data:/app/data
    # Named volume alternative:
    # - mydata:/app/data
volumes:
  mydata:

title: Docker: out of disk space / no space left
solution_steps: 1. `docker system df` to see disk usage
2. `docker system prune -a --volumes` to clean everything
3. `docker image prune -a` to remove unused images
4. `docker builder prune` to clean build cache
5. Increase disk space in Docker Desktop settings
6. Move Docker data directory to a larger drive
7. Run `docker system df` before and after cleanup
code_example:
docker system df
# Cleanup all unused
docker system prune -a --volumes -f
# Or selective
docker image prune -a -f
docker container prune -f
docker builder prune -f

title: Docker: environment variables not passed
solution_steps: 1. Use `-e VAR=value` with `docker run`
2. Use `env_file: .env` in docker-compose
3. Use `environment:` section in docker-compose
4. Use `ARG` in Dockerfile for build-time, `ENV` for runtime
5. Check variable expansion: `${VAR}` vs `$VAR`
6. Verify with `docker inspect <container>`
7. Never hardcode secrets — use Docker secrets or `.env`
code_example:
# docker-compose.yml
services:
  app:
    image: myapp
    env_file: .env
    environment:
      - NODE_ENV=production
      - DB_URL=${DB_URL:-postgres://localhost/default}
    # Note: .env file is auto-loaded by docker-compose if in same directory

title: Docker: conflicting container names
error_patterns: is already in use by container
solution_steps: 1. Remove existing container: `docker rm <container>`
2. Use different name: `docker run --name myapp2 ...`
3. Use `--rm` flag to auto-remove on stop
4. Check existing: `docker ps -a | grep <name>`
5. Use docker-compose which manages naming automatically
code_example:
docker rm -f my-container
# Or rename
docker rename old-name new-name
# Use --rm to auto-clean
docker run --rm --name myapp myimage

title: Docker: secrets and sensitive data in images
solution_steps: 1. Never use `ENV` for secrets in Dockerfile
2. Use multi-stage builds to avoid secrets in final image
3. Use `--secret` build flag (BuildKit): `--secret id=mysecret,src=./secret.txt`
4. Use Docker Compose `secrets:` section
5. Use `.dockerignore` to exclude credentials
6. Scan images with `docker scout` or `trivy` for leaked secrets
code_example:
# Dockerfile (BuildKit)
# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1.4
RUN --mount=type=secret,id=api_key \
    export API_KEY=$(cat /run/secrets/api_key) && \
    ./build.sh

# Build command:
# DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build --secret id=api_key,src=./api_key.txt .

title: Docker: WSL integration issues on Windows
solution_steps: 1. Enable WSL 2 backend in Docker Desktop Settings
2. Ensure WSL 2 is installed: `wsl --set-version <distro> 2`
3. Restart Docker Desktop and WSL: `wsl --shutdown`
4. Check Docker Desktop → Resources → WSL Integration
5. Reinstall Docker Desktop if WSL integration fails
6. Ensure virtualization is enabled in BIOS
code_example:
# From PowerShell (Admin)
wsl --set-version ubuntu 2
wsl --shutdown
# Verify WSL 2
wsl -l -v

title: Docker: non-root user best practices
solution_steps: 1. Add `USER` directive in Dockerfile after installing packages
2. Create user: `RUN addgroup -S appgroup && adduser -S appuser -G appgroup`
3. Use `USER 1000:1000` (avoiding named user/groups)
4. Ensure app directory has correct ownership
5. Use different users for different services
6. For bind mounts, match UID/GID with host user
code_example:
FROM node:18-alpine
RUN addgroup -S appgroup && adduser -S appuser -G appgroup
WORKDIR /app
COPY --chown=appuser:appgroup . .
USER appuser
CMD ["node", "server.js"]

title: Docker Compose: restart policies
solution_steps: 1. Use `restart: no` (default) — no automatic restart
2. Use `restart: always` — restart regardless of exit code
3. Use `restart: on-failure` — only restart on non-zero exit codes
4. Use `restart: unless-stopped` — restart unless explicitly stopped
5. Add healthchecks to prevent restart loops on boot
6. Set `max_attempts` (Docker swarm only)
code_example:
services:
  app:
    image: myapp
    restart: unless-stopped
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost/health"]
      interval: 30s
      retries: 3

title: Docker: .dockerignore not working
solution_steps: 1. Create `.dockerignore` in the same directory as Dockerfile
2. Use patterns: `node_modules`, `.git`, `*.log`, `__pycache__`
3. Each line is a glob pattern (similar to .gitignore)
4. Use `!` for negation: `!important/file.txt`
5. Check build context: run without .dockerignore and compare size
6. Note: .dockerignore affects build context, not layers
code_example:
# .dockerignore
node_modules
.git
.env
*.log
__pycache__
.vscode
.idea
*.md

title: Docker: container DNS resolution failure
solution_steps: 1. Configure DNS in daemon.json: `{"dns": ["8.8.8.8"]}`
2. Use `--dns` flag: `docker run --dns 8.8.8.8 ...`
3. Check `/etc/resolv.conf` inside container
4. Check host machine DNS resolution
5. Restart Docker Desktop
6. For Docker Compose: add `dns:` section to service
code_example:
services:
  app:
    image: myapp
    dns:
      - 8.8.8.8
      - 1.1.1.1
    dns_search: .

# Or run with
docker run --dns 8.8.8.8 --dns 1.1.1.1 myimage

title: Docker: multi-platform builds
solution_steps: 1. Use `docker buildx create --use` to create multi-arch builder
2. Build with: `docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 -t image .`
3. Push multi-arch manifest: `--push` flag
4. Use QEMU for emulation: `docker run --privileged --rm tonistiigi/binfmt --install all`
5. Check architecture: `uname -m` inside container
6. For ARM builds on x86: enable in Docker Desktop experimental settings
code_example:
docker buildx create --name mybuilder --use
docker buildx build \
  --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64 \
  -t myuser/myapp:latest \
  --push .

title: Docker Compose: networking between services
solution_steps: 1. Services communicate by service name (not localhost)
2. Use `service_name:port` format for inter-service calls
3. Custom networks: define `networks:` section in compose
4. External networks: `network: external` for shared networks
5. Use `depends_on` for startup ordering
6. Check `docker-compose ps` for service status
code_example:
services:
  api:
    image: myapi
    ports: ["8080:8080"]
    networks: [app-net]
  db:
    image: postgres
    networks: [app-net]

networks:
  app-net:
    driver: bridge

# api connects via: postgresql://db:5432/mydb

title: Docker: TMPFS and temporary storage
solution_steps: 1. Mount tmpfs: `docker run --tmpfs /app/tmp:noexec,nosuid,size=64M`
2. In compose: `tmpfs: /app/tmp:noexec,nosuid,size=64M`
3. Use for /tmp to avoid writing to container layer
4. Set size limits to avoid filling up memory
5. Data is lost when container stops (by design)
code_example:
services:
  app:
    image: myapp
    tmpfs:
      - /tmp:noexec,nosuid,size=128M
    # Or using volumes
    volumes:
      - type: tmpfs
        target: /tmp
        tmpfs:
          size: 128M

title: Docker: health check not working
solution_steps: 1. Ensure healthcheck command returns 0 for healthy, 1 for unhealthy
2. Use `curl`, `wget`, or custom script in healthcheck
3. Set proper `interval`, `timeout`, and `retries`
4. Check that the app responds on the expected port before healthcheck runs
5. Test healthcheck command manually: `docker exec <container> curl -f http://localhost/health`
6. Use `start_period` to give app time to start before health checks
code_example:
FROM node:18
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=10s --start-period=5s --retries=3 \
  CMD curl -f http://localhost:3000/health || exit 1

# In docker-compose
healthcheck:
  test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:3000/health"]
  interval: 30s
  timeout: 10s
  retries: 3

title: Docker Compose: extends and sharing config
solution_steps: 1. Use YAML anchors `&` and references `*` for DRY configs
2. Use `extends` (Compose v2) or YAML merge `<<` (v3+)
3. Create base compose file and override with profile-specific files
4. Use `docker-compose -f base.yml -f override.yml`
5. Use environment variable interpolation in compose files
code_example:
services:
  app: &app-base
    image: myapp
    environment: &env-base
      NODE_ENV: production
      LOG_LEVEL: info

  worker:
    <<: *app-base
    command: npm run worker
    environment:
      <<: *env-base
      QUEUE: processing
